1:
"And Balaam said unto Balak, Build me here seven altars, and prepare me here seven oxen and seven rams." The witnesses note Balaam directing the building of seven altars and the preparing of seven oxen and rams, on a high place of Baal. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was upon one of the high places of Baal, after Balaam had viewed Israel encamped. The witnesses note that Henry marks the great preparation made for the cursing of Israel, aiming to engage the God of Israel to forsake them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven altars — the costly preparation for cursing; the oxen and rams — victims to engage the God of Israel; the a high place of Baal — the idolatrous height chosen; the the folly of it — imagining God would be at men's beck.

The puritan expositor (Henry) exposes the folly of the design: by costly sacrifices on seven altars Balak and Balaam thought to bribe or persuade the God of Israel to abandon his people, or at least to stand neutral — "the sottishness of superstition, to imagine that God will be at men's beck"; the whole apparatus of altars and victims was an attempt to bend the Almighty to their malice, as if the Maker of Israel could be hired to curse his own.

So the witnesses make the verse the seven altars: The witnesses note the seven altars: the costly preparation for cursing. The witnesses note the oxen and rams: victims to engage the God of Israel. The witnesses note the a high place of Baal: the idolatrous height chosen. The witnesses note the the folly of it: imagining God would be at men's beck. The expositors observe the seven altars: the costly preparation for cursing. The expositors observe the oxen and rams: victims to engage the God of Israel. The expositors observe the a high place of Baal: the idolatrous height chosen. The expositors observe the the folly of it: imagining God would be at men's beck. The believer is taught to mark the seven altars, the oxen and rams, the a high place of Baal, the the folly of it, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam ordered seven altars and sacrifices to curse Israel — the sottishness of superstition, imagining the God of Israel could be hired to forsake his own.
2:
"And Balak did as Balaam had spoken; and Balak and Balaam offered on every altar a bullock and a ram." The witnesses note Balak doing as directed, the two offering a bullock and a ram on every altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balak ordered the seven altars built and the sacrifices prepared. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set both offering on every altar a bullock and a ram. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Balak did as spoken — obeying the soothsayer exactly; the on every altar — a bullock and a ram each; the no cost spared — lavishing sacrifice in his enmity; the zeal in evil — earnest malice against God's people.

The witnesses agree the king spared no cost in his enmity: he did exactly as the soothsayer bade, lavishing bullocks and rams on every altar, that the whole might move God against Israel; the readiness of Balak to pour out such sacrifice in the cause of cursing shows how earnest the wicked can be in their malice, sparing no expense to compass the hurt of God's people — a zeal in evil that shames the coldness of many in good.

So the witnesses make the verse the sacrifices offered: The witnesses note the Balak did as spoken: obeying the soothsayer exactly. The witnesses note the on every altar: a bullock and a ram each. The witnesses note the no cost spared: lavishing sacrifice in his enmity. The witnesses note the zeal in evil: earnest malice against God's people. The expositors observe the Balak did as spoken: obeying the soothsayer exactly. The expositors observe the on every altar: a bullock and a ram each. The expositors observe the no cost spared: lavishing sacrifice in his enmity. The expositors observe the zeal in evil: earnest malice against God's people. The believer is taught to mark the Balak did as spoken, the on every altar, the no cost spared, the zeal in evil, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balak offered bullocks and rams on every altar — sparing no cost in his enmity, a zeal in evil that shames the coldness of many in good.
3:
"And Balaam said unto Balak, Stand by thy burnt offering, and I will go: peradventure the LORD will come to meet me..." The witnesses note Balaam bidding Balak stand by the offering while he went apart, hoping the LORD would meet him. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balak was to stand presenting himself and his offering to the Lord, that he might have respect to both. The witnesses note that Balaam went to seek what the LORD would show him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stand by the offering — balak presenting himself and his sacrifice; the I will go — balaam going apart to seek God; the peradventure — the diviner's helpless uncertainty; the God to overrule — meeting Balaam not to gratify but to overrule him.

The witnesses agree the scene mingles superstition with a true sense of God's sovereignty: Balaam, the hireling diviner, yet knew that he could say nothing but what the LORD gave him, and so went apart "peradventure the LORD will come to meet me"; the uncertainty of his "peradventure" betrays that even the enchanter's arts were helpless before God, who would meet Balaam not to gratify his malice but to overrule it, putting his own word in the prophet's mouth.

So the witnesses make the verse seeking the LORD: The witnesses note the stand by the offering: Balak presenting himself and his sacrifice. The witnesses note the I will go: Balaam going apart to seek God. The witnesses note the peradventure: the diviner's helpless uncertainty. The witnesses note the God to overrule: meeting Balaam not to gratify but to overrule him. The expositors observe the stand by the offering: Balak presenting himself and his sacrifice. The expositors observe the I will go: Balaam going apart to seek God. The expositors observe the peradventure: the diviner's helpless uncertainty. The expositors observe the God to overrule: meeting Balaam not to gratify but to overrule him. The believer is taught to mark the stand by the offering, the I will go, the peradventure, the God to overrule, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam went apart hoping the LORD would meet him — the diviner helpless before God, who would meet him not to gratify his malice but to overrule it.
4:
"And God met Balaam: and he said unto him, I have prepared seven altars, and I have offered upon every altar a bullock and a ram." The witnesses note God meeting Balaam — not in favour, but to counterwork the designs against Israel. The witnesses note that Gill stresses God met him not in a kind or gracious manner, not out of respect to him or his offerings, but for the sake of his people Israel, to confound the designs of their enemies. The witnesses note that Balaam reported his seven altars and offerings, as if to claim a hearing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the God met him — but not in favour or grace; the for Israel's sake — to counterwork their enemies; the not for his offerings — no respect to the sacrifices; the overruling the wicked — the hired mouth turned to blessing.

The witnesses agree the meeting was an act of God's sovereign care for Israel, not of regard for Balaam: the LORD came to the diviner not to commune with him as a friend nor to accept his sacrifices, but to seize his tongue and turn his intended curse into blessing; that God should "meet" even a Balaam shows how he overrules the wicked for the good of his own, making the very mouth hired to curse them the instrument of their blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse God met Balaam: The witnesses note the God met him: but not in favour or grace. The witnesses note the for Israel's sake: to counterwork their enemies. The witnesses note the not for his offerings: no respect to the sacrifices. The witnesses note the overruling the wicked: the hired mouth turned to blessing. The expositors observe the God met him: but not in favour or grace. The expositors observe the for Israel's sake: to counterwork their enemies. The expositors observe the not for his offerings: no respect to the sacrifices. The expositors observe the overruling the wicked: the hired mouth turned to blessing. The believer is taught to mark the God met him, the for Israel's sake, the not for his offerings, the overruling the wicked, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that God met Balaam, not in favour but for Israel's sake — overruling the wicked, making the very mouth hired to curse the instrument of blessing.
5:
"And the LORD put a word in Balaam's mouth, and said, Return unto Balak, and thus thou shalt speak." The witnesses note the LORD putting his word in Balaam's mouth, that he must speak it whether he would or no. The witnesses note that Gill notes God put not grace into his heart nor the fear of God within him, but suggested what to say, impressing it with such power and weight that he was obliged to deliver it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the LORD bidding him return and speak. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the put a word — god controlling his speech; the not grace in his heart — the man unchanged, only constrained; the thus thou shalt speak — obliged to deliver God's word; the the unwilling mouthpiece — god using a wicked instrument for his truth.

The witnesses agree the LORD made the unwilling prophet his mouthpiece: he put the word in Balaam's mouth not by changing his heart — which remained covetous and hostile — but by an overruling power that he could not resist, so that the diviner must utter God's blessing against his own wish; the case shows that God can use even a wicked instrument to declare his truth, the word controlling the man though the man loved it not.

So the witnesses make the verse the word put in his mouth: The witnesses note the put a word: God controlling his speech. The witnesses note the not grace in his heart: the man unchanged, only constrained. The witnesses note the thus thou shalt speak: obliged to deliver God's word. The witnesses note the the unwilling mouthpiece: God using a wicked instrument for his truth. The expositors observe the put a word: God controlling his speech. The expositors observe the not grace in his heart: the man unchanged, only constrained. The expositors observe the thus thou shalt speak: obliged to deliver God's word. The expositors observe the the unwilling mouthpiece: God using a wicked instrument for his truth. The believer is taught to mark the put a word, the not grace in his heart, the thus thou shalt speak, the the unwilling mouthpiece, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the LORD put his word in Balaam's mouth, constraining the unwilling prophet — God using even a wicked instrument to declare his truth, the word controlling the man.
6:
"And he returned unto him, and, lo, he stood by his burnt sacrifice, he, and all the princes of Moab." The witnesses note Balaam returning at once to Balak, who stood waiting with all the princes of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes he returned immediately, as soon as he was told what to say. The witnesses note that Balak and all the princes stood by the offering, awaiting the curse. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the returned at once — balaam coming straight back; the stood by his sacrifice — balak waiting by the altars; the all the princes of Moab — the whole company expectant; the awaiting a curse — poised to hear a doom that would not come.

The witnesses agree the eager assembly waited for a curse they would not get: Balak and the whole company of Moab's princes stood expectant by the smoking altars, sure that the famous diviner would bring down a curse on Israel; the scene sets the stage for the great reversal, the gathered enemies of God's people poised to hear their doom pronounced, only to hear instead the LORD's blessing fall from the very lips they had hired.

So the witnesses make the verse the waiting princes: The witnesses note the returned at once: Balaam coming straight back. The witnesses note the stood by his sacrifice: Balak waiting by the altars. The witnesses note the all the princes of Moab: the whole company expectant. The witnesses note the awaiting a curse: poised to hear a doom that would not come. The expositors observe the returned at once: Balaam coming straight back. The expositors observe the stood by his sacrifice: Balak waiting by the altars. The expositors observe the all the princes of Moab: the whole company expectant. The expositors observe the awaiting a curse: poised to hear a doom that would not come. The believer is taught to mark the returned at once, the stood by his sacrifice, the all the princes of Moab, the awaiting a curse, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam returned to Balak and the waiting princes — the gathered enemies poised to hear Israel cursed, only to hear the LORD's blessing from the hired lips.
7:
"And he took up his parable, and said, Balak the king of Moab hath brought me from Aram, out of the mountains of the east, saying, Come, curse me Jacob, and come, defy Israel." The witnesses note Balaam taking up his parable, recounting how Balak had fetched him from the east to curse Jacob. The witnesses note that Gill notes the prophetic word is called a "parable" because prophecies often use figurative, weighty, and sententious expressions. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the opening — Balak fetched him from Aram, the mountains of the east, to curse Jacob. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took up his parable — the weighty prophetic word; the brought me from Aram — fetched from the east to curse; the curse me Jacob — the errand of Balak; the the reversal prepared — the curser to publish a blessing.

The witnesses agree the oracle opens by laying bare the errand and its futility: Balaam rehearses that Balak brought him a long way to curse Israel, the very rehearsal preparing the reversal — for what follows shows the errand vain; the solemn "parable" form, weighty and elevated, befits a word that is not Balaam's but God's, the hired curser made to publish, in lofty prophetic speech, the blessedness of those he came to curse.

So the witnesses make the verse the parable taken up: The witnesses note the took up his parable: the weighty prophetic word. The witnesses note the brought me from Aram: fetched from the east to curse. The witnesses note the curse me Jacob: the errand of Balak. The witnesses note the the reversal prepared: the curser to publish a blessing. The expositors observe the took up his parable: the weighty prophetic word. The expositors observe the brought me from Aram: fetched from the east to curse. The expositors observe the curse me Jacob: the errand of Balak. The expositors observe the the reversal prepared: the curser to publish a blessing. The believer is taught to mark the took up his parable, the brought me from Aram, the curse me Jacob, the the reversal prepared, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam took up his parable, rehearsing Balak's errand to curse Jacob — the solemn form befitting God's word, the hired curser made to publish the blessedness of those he came to curse.
8:
"How shall I curse, whom God hath not cursed? or how shall I defy, whom the LORD hath not defied?" The witnesses note the heart of the first oracle — Balaam cannot curse whom God has not cursed. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam had the will to curse, to gain Balak's money and honour, but saw it impossible — God does not curse his spiritual Israel, who are blessed in Christ. The witnesses note that it was vain to attempt what God would not have done. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the how shall I curse — the will to curse made helpless; the whom God hath not cursed — the divine will unmovable; the blessed in Christ — the spiritual Israel beyond cursing; the none can charge them — if God be for us (Rom. 8:31-33).

The witnesses agree this is the great principle of the chapter and the comfort of the church: no curse can fall on those whom God has blessed, for the enemy's malice cannot move the divine will; Gill turns it to the spiritual Israel — blessed in Christ, they cannot be cursed, and the apostle echoes the truth, "if God be for us, who can be against us?... who shall lay anything to the charge of God's elect?" (Rom. 8:31-33); the would-be curser confesses his arts are powerless against a people God has not cursed.

So the witnesses make the verse no curse on the blessed: The witnesses note the how shall I curse: the will to curse made helpless. The witnesses note the whom God hath not cursed: the divine will unmovable. The witnesses note the blessed in Christ: the spiritual Israel beyond cursing. The witnesses note the none can charge them: if God be for us (Rom. 8:31-33). The expositors observe the how shall I curse: the will to curse made helpless. The expositors observe the whom God hath not cursed: the divine will unmovable. The expositors observe the blessed in Christ: the spiritual Israel beyond cursing. The expositors observe the none can charge them: if God be for us (Rom. 8:31-33). The believer is taught to mark the how shall I curse, the whom God hath not cursed, the blessed in Christ, the none can charge them, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam could not curse whom God had not cursed — no curse falling on the blessed, the comfort of the church, that none can charge whom God has justified (Rom. 8:31-33).
9:
"For from the top of the rocks I see him, and from the hills I behold him: lo, the people shall dwell alone, and shall not be reckoned among the nations." The witnesses note Balaam beholding Israel from the heights and declaring them a people that dwells alone, set apart from the nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam, on the high hill, had a view of Israel encamped in the plains below. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the people dwelling alone, not reckoned among the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from the top of the rocks — balaam beholding the camp below; the the people dwell alone — set apart from the nations; the not reckoned among them — distinct, the LORD's own; the separation their glory — a peculiar people, not of the world (1 Pet. 2:9).

The witnesses agree the oracle marks Israel's distinctness as the ground of their security: looking down on the ordered camp, Balaam saw a people unlike the rest, "dwelling alone," separated to God and not numbered with the heathen; the separation that the world counts strange is in truth their glory and their safety, a people set apart for the LORD's own possession, kept distinct that they might be holy — a calling fulfilled in the church, a peculiar people, not of the world (cf. 1 Pet. 2:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the people that dwells alone: The witnesses note the from the top of the rocks: Balaam beholding the camp below. The witnesses note the the people dwell alone: set apart from the nations. The witnesses note the not reckoned among them: distinct, the LORD's own. The witnesses note the separation their glory: a peculiar people, not of the world (1 Pet. 2:9). The expositors observe the from the top of the rocks: Balaam beholding the camp below. The expositors observe the the people dwell alone: set apart from the nations. The expositors observe the not reckoned among them: distinct, the LORD's own. The expositors observe the separation their glory: a peculiar people, not of the world (1 Pet. 2:9). The believer is taught to mark the from the top of the rocks, the the people dwell alone, the not reckoned among them, the separation their glory, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam saw Israel a people dwelling alone, not reckoned among the nations — their distinctness their glory and safety, a peculiar people set apart for God (1 Pet. 2:9).
10:
"Who can count the dust of Jacob, and the number of the fourth part of Israel? Let me die the death of the righteous, and let my last end be like his!" The witnesses note Israel's countless multitude, and Balaam's famous wish — to die the death of the righteous. The witnesses note that Gill notes the dust of Jacob speaks of their numbers, as numerous as the dust of the earth, fulfilling the promise. The witnesses note that Clarke judges Balaam had some presentiment that he should be cut off by a violent death, and wished rather to die the death of the righteous. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the dust of Jacob — the countless multitude of Israel; the let me die their death — longing for the saint's happy end; the but not their life — clinging to covetousness and curse; the a divided heart — coveting the reward, refusing the way (Num. 31:8).

The witnesses agree the wish is one of the most tragic words in Scripture: Balaam, beholding the blessedness of God's people, longed to share their happy end — "let me die the death of the righteous" — yet would not live the life of the righteous, clinging to his covetousness and his curse; the desire to die well without the will to live well is the very picture of a divided heart, coveting the saint's reward while refusing the saint's way, and Balaam himself died at last by the sword among the enemies of God (cf. Num. 31:8).

So the witnesses make the verse the death of the righteous: The witnesses note the the dust of Jacob: the countless multitude of Israel. The witnesses note the let me die their death: longing for the saint's happy end. The witnesses note the but not their life: clinging to covetousness and curse. The witnesses note the a divided heart: coveting the reward, refusing the way (Num. 31:8). The expositors observe the the dust of Jacob: the countless multitude of Israel. The expositors observe the let me die their death: longing for the saint's happy end. The expositors observe the but not their life: clinging to covetousness and curse. The expositors observe the a divided heart: coveting the reward, refusing the way (Num. 31:8). The believer is taught to mark the the dust of Jacob, the let me die their death, the but not their life, the a divided heart, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam wished to die the death of the righteous yet would not live their life — the divided heart coveting the saint's end while refusing his way, dying at last by the sword (Num. 31:8).
11:
"And Balak said unto Balaam, What hast thou done unto me? I took thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast blessed them altogether." The witnesses note Balak's angry reproach — he hired Balaam to curse his enemies, and Balaam has wholly blessed them. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balak asks "what hast thou done?" — nothing to answer his purpose. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads the Hebrew "thou hast blessed, to bless," an emphatic statement that Balaam had uttered nothing but blessings; Calvin notes the proud man reproaching the false prophet as if he had purchased a curse. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the what hast thou done — the angry complaint; the I took thee to curse — the hired purpose frustrated; the blessed them altogether — nothing but blessing uttered; the the scheme recoiled — the intended curse turned wholly to blessing.

The witnesses agree the reversal was complete and galling to Balak: he had paid for a curse and received a benediction, the very opposite of his desire, "blessed them altogether"; Calvin marks the indignation of the proud king who thought he had bought a curse with his sacrifices, only to find the diviner's mouth full of blessing — a vivid proof that the schemes of the wicked against God's people recoil, the intended curse turned wholly to blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse Balak's reproach: The witnesses note the what hast thou done: the angry complaint. The witnesses note the I took thee to curse: the hired purpose frustrated. The witnesses note the blessed them altogether: nothing but blessing uttered. The witnesses note the the scheme recoiled: the intended curse turned wholly to blessing. The expositors observe the what hast thou done: the angry complaint. The expositors observe the I took thee to curse: the hired purpose frustrated. The expositors observe the blessed them altogether: nothing but blessing uttered. The expositors observe the the scheme recoiled: the intended curse turned wholly to blessing. The believer is taught to mark the what hast thou done, the I took thee to curse, the blessed them altogether, the the scheme recoiled, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balak reproached Balaam for blessing his enemies altogether — the hired curse turned wholly to blessing, the schemes of the wicked recoiling against them.
12:
"And he answered and said, Must I not take heed to speak that which the LORD hath put in my mouth?" The witnesses note Balaam's answer — he must speak only what the LORD puts in his mouth. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam replied to Balak by way of defence. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read his words — must he not take heed to speak what the LORD put in his mouth? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the must I not take heed — bound to speak God's word; the that which the LORD put — the word not at his disposal; the a true principle — spoken from a false heart; the a reluctant witness — owning he cannot speak against God's purpose.

The witnesses agree the diviner spoke a true principle even from a false heart: bound by God's overruling power, he could utter nothing but the word given him, and so confessed he "must" speak it; the irony is sharp — the covetous prophet, who would gladly have cursed for hire, is made to own that the word of the LORD is not at his disposal, a reluctant witness to the truth that no man can make God's mouthpiece speak against God's purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse the constrained prophet: The witnesses note the must I not take heed: bound to speak God's word. The witnesses note the that which the LORD put: the word not at his disposal. The witnesses note the a true principle: spoken from a false heart. The witnesses note the a reluctant witness: owning he cannot speak against God's purpose. The expositors observe the must I not take heed: bound to speak God's word. The expositors observe the that which the LORD put: the word not at his disposal. The expositors observe the a true principle: spoken from a false heart. The expositors observe the a reluctant witness: owning he cannot speak against God's purpose. The believer is taught to mark the must I not take heed, the that which the LORD put, the a true principle, the a reluctant witness, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam owned he must speak only what the LORD put in his mouth — a true principle from a false heart, a reluctant witness that God's mouthpiece cannot speak against his purpose.
13:
"And Balak said unto him, Come, I pray thee, with me unto another place... thou shalt see but the utmost part of them, and shalt not see them all; and curse me them from thence." The witnesses note Balak trying again — taking Balaam to another place to see only part of Israel, hoping for a curse from thence. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balak, unable to help himself, was willing to try Balaam another time and elsewhere. The witnesses note that Henry marks the second preparation for cursing, the place changed, Balak fancying the full prospect of the camp had hindered the curse. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unto another place — balak shifting the ground; the but the utmost part — to see only the edge of Israel; the curse them from thence — hoping a new vantage would help; the the superstitious fancy — as if God's will turned on the prophet's view.

The puritan expositor (Henry) notes the superstitious fancy behind the move: Balak imagined that the sight of all Israel had somehow overawed Balaam, and that from a spot where he saw only the outskirts he might more easily curse them; the folly supposes that God's blessing depends on the diviner's vantage, when in truth no change of place could change the divine will — the king grasping at any expedient rather than yield his malice.

So the witnesses make the verse another place tried: The witnesses note the unto another place: Balak shifting the ground. The witnesses note the but the utmost part: to see only the edge of Israel. The witnesses note the curse them from thence: hoping a new vantage would help. The witnesses note the the superstitious fancy: as if God's will turned on the prophet's view. The expositors observe the unto another place: Balak shifting the ground. The expositors observe the but the utmost part: to see only the edge of Israel. The expositors observe the curse them from thence: hoping a new vantage would help. The expositors observe the the superstitious fancy: as if God's will turned on the prophet's view. The believer is taught to mark the unto another place, the but the utmost part, the curse them from thence, the the superstitious fancy, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balak took Balaam to another place to see but part of Israel and curse them — the superstitious fancy that God's will could change with the prophet's vantage.
14:
"And he brought him into the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah, and built seven altars, and offered a bullock and a ram on every altar." The witnesses note the move to the field of Zophim, the top of Pisgah, with seven more altars and sacrifices. The witnesses note that Gill notes the field of Zophim was named from the watchmen, a high place for viewing armies. The witnesses note that Ellicott identifies it near the spot from which Moses afterward surveyed the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the top of Pisgah — the watchmen's height chosen; the seven altars again — the same costly ritual repeated; the a bullock and a ram — the sacrifices renewed; the vain persistence — as if expense could wring a curse from God.

The witnesses agree the same costly ritual was repeated in vain hope: another height, another seven altars, another set of victims, as if persistence or expense could wring a curse from God; the repetition shows both the obstinacy of Balak's malice and the emptiness of his means — for the God of Israel is not moved by the multiplying of altars, and the second attempt would prove as fruitless as the first, the blessing only confirmed.

So the witnesses make the verse the field of Zophim: The witnesses note the the top of Pisgah: the watchmen's height chosen. The witnesses note the seven altars again: the same costly ritual repeated. The witnesses note the a bullock and a ram: the sacrifices renewed. The witnesses note the vain persistence: as if expense could wring a curse from God. The expositors observe the the top of Pisgah: the watchmen's height chosen. The expositors observe the seven altars again: the same costly ritual repeated. The expositors observe the a bullock and a ram: the sacrifices renewed. The expositors observe the vain persistence: as if expense could wring a curse from God. The believer is taught to mark the the top of Pisgah, the seven altars again, the a bullock and a ram, the vain persistence, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balak built seven more altars on Pisgah — the same costly ritual repeated in vain, the God of Israel not moved by the multiplying of altars.
15:
"And he said unto Balak, Stand here by thy burnt offering, while I meet the LORD yonder." The witnesses note Balaam again bidding Balak stand by the offering while he went to meet the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was as he had directed before. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read him going to meet the LORD yonder while Balak stood by his offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stand by thy offering — balak posted as before; the I meet the LORD — balaam going apart again; the the same ceremony — the repetition underlining the futility; the the result fixed — the LORD's purpose toward Israel unchanged.

The witnesses agree the very repetition underlines the futility of the enterprise: the same ceremony, the same posture, the same going apart "to meet the LORD" — yet the result could not differ, for the LORD's purpose toward Israel was fixed; Balaam's confident "while I meet the LORD yonder" is now less a peradventure than an expectation, the diviner himself by this point half resigned that he will hear nothing but blessing, the second meeting as bound as the first.

So the witnesses make the verse meeting the LORD again: The witnesses note the stand by thy offering: Balak posted as before. The witnesses note the I meet the LORD: Balaam going apart again. The witnesses note the the same ceremony: the repetition underlining the futility. The witnesses note the the result fixed: the LORD's purpose toward Israel unchanged. The expositors observe the stand by thy offering: Balak posted as before. The expositors observe the I meet the LORD: Balaam going apart again. The expositors observe the the same ceremony: the repetition underlining the futility. The expositors observe the the result fixed: the LORD's purpose toward Israel unchanged. The believer is taught to mark the stand by thy offering, the I meet the LORD, the the same ceremony, the the result fixed, and to rest in the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Balaam again went apart to meet the LORD — the same ceremony repeated, the result fixed, the LORD's purpose toward Israel unchanged.
16:
"And the LORD met Balaam, and put a word in his mouth, and said, Go again unto Balak, and say thus." The witnesses note the LORD again meeting Balaam and putting his word in his mouth. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was as he did before — the LORD met him and put a word in his mouth. The witnesses note that the second meeting was as sovereign as the first. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD met Balaam — again, as sovereign as before; the put a word in his mouth — god controlling the prophet's tongue; the go again unto Balak — sent back with God's word; the nothing changed — the same God, the same purpose.

The witnesses agree the repetition confirms God's unchanging control of the prophet's tongue: a second time the LORD met Balaam and gave him his word, that the second oracle, like the first, should be God's and not man's; the diviner's change of place changed nothing, for the same God met him with the same purpose, and the blessing was about to be not only repeated but enlarged and sealed.

So the witnesses make the verse the second meeting: The witnesses note the the LORD met Balaam: again, as sovereign as before. The witnesses note the put a word in his mouth: God controlling the prophet's tongue. The witnesses note the go again unto Balak: sent back with God's word. The witnesses note the nothing changed: the same God, the same purpose. The expositors observe the the LORD met Balaam: again, as sovereign as before. The expositors observe the put a word in his mouth: God controlling the prophet's tongue. The expositors observe the go again unto Balak: sent back with God's word. The expositors observe the nothing changed: the same God, the same purpose. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD met Balaam, the put a word in his mouth, the go again unto Balak, the nothing changed, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that the LORD met Balaam again and put his word in his mouth — the change of place changing nothing, the second oracle as much God's as the first.
17:
"And when he came to him, behold, he stood by his burnt offering, and the princes of Moab with him. And Balak said unto him, What hath the LORD spoken?" The witnesses note Balak, now resigned, asking not for a curse but "what hath the LORD spoken?" The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes that before it was "all the princes," but now only some, for seeing no hope of success, some had gone away. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Balak now understood Balaam was wholly under the influence of Jehovah and would say nothing but what God gave him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stood by his offering — waiting again by the altars; the the princes with him — but fewer now, some gone in despair; the what hath the LORD spoken — owning the matter lay with Jehovah; the the malice breaking up — moab's cause failing before God's purpose.

The witnesses agree the king's question betrays a dawning surrender: where before he demanded a curse, now he asks only "what hath the LORD spoken?" — owning, at last, that the matter lay with Jehovah and not with the diviner; and the thinning of the princes, some slipping away as hope failed, shows the cause of Moab's malice already breaking up before the immovable purpose of God to bless his people.

So the witnesses make the verse Balak's surrender: The witnesses note the stood by his offering: waiting again by the altars. The witnesses note the the princes with him: but fewer now, some gone in despair. The witnesses note the what hath the LORD spoken: owning the matter lay with Jehovah. The witnesses note the the malice breaking up: Moab's cause failing before God's purpose. The expositors observe the stood by his offering: waiting again by the altars. The expositors observe the the princes with him: but fewer now, some gone in despair. The expositors observe the what hath the LORD spoken: owning the matter lay with Jehovah. The expositors observe the the malice breaking up: Moab's cause failing before God's purpose. The believer is taught to mark the stood by his offering, the the princes with him, the what hath the LORD spoken, the the malice breaking up, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balak now asked "what hath the LORD spoken?" — a dawning surrender, owning the matter lay with Jehovah, Moab's malice breaking up before God's purpose to bless.
18:
"And he took up his parable, and said, Rise up, Balak, and hear; hearken unto me, thou son of Zippor:" The witnesses note the second parable opening with a solemn call to Balak to rise and hear. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam pronounced the word put into his mouth. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the elevated language and the emphatic preface "Rise up, Balak, and hear," repetitions indicating something uncommon and awakening the hearer's attention. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took up his parable — the second weighty oracle; the rise up, Balak — the emphatic call to attention; the hearken unto me — demanding the king's full heed; the a solemn declaration — preparing a truth Balak will not want to hear.

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the weight of the summons: the doubled call to attention — "Rise up... hearken" — signals that what follows is no ordinary word but a solemn declaration of God's mind, demanding the king's full heed; the very form prepares Balak for a truth he will not want to hear, that the God of Israel is unchangeable in his blessing, and bids the proud son of Zippor stand and receive it.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn summons: The witnesses note the took up his parable: the second weighty oracle. The witnesses note the rise up, Balak: the emphatic call to attention. The witnesses note the hearken unto me: demanding the king's full heed. The witnesses note the a solemn declaration: preparing a truth Balak will not want to hear. The expositors observe the took up his parable: the second weighty oracle. The expositors observe the rise up, Balak: the emphatic call to attention. The expositors observe the hearken unto me: demanding the king's full heed. The expositors observe the a solemn declaration: preparing a truth Balak will not want to hear. The believer is taught to mark the took up his parable, the rise up, Balak, the hearken unto me, the a solemn declaration, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that the second parable opened with a solemn call to Balak to rise and hear — the weighty form preparing the king for the unwelcome truth of God's unchangeable blessing.
19:
"God is not a man, that he should lie; neither the son of man, that he should repent: hath he said, and shall he not do it? or hath he spoken, and shall he not make it good?" The witnesses note the great declaration of God's truth and unchangeableness — he is not a man, that he should lie or repent. The witnesses note that Gill notes God is not to be conceived of in a corporeal manner, nor as a sinful man who goes astray and speaks lies; let God be true and every man a liar. The witnesses note that what he has said he will surely do, what he has spoken he will make good. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not a man to lie — the faithfulness of God; the nor to repent — his purpose unchanging; the hath he said — what he speaks he surely does; the the believer's rock — impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2).

The witnesses agree this verse is the rock under all God's promises and the death of Balak's hope: God cannot lie nor change his mind as men do, and so the blessing he has spoken on Israel he will certainly perform; the truth that "God is not a man, that he should lie" is the believer's strong consolation, for it is impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2), and what he has promised he is able and faithful to do — no enemy's wish can move the word of the unchanging God.

So the witnesses make the verse God is not a man: The witnesses note the not a man to lie: the faithfulness of God. The witnesses note the nor to repent: his purpose unchanging. The witnesses note the hath he said: what he speaks he surely does. The witnesses note the the believer's rock: impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2). The expositors observe the not a man to lie: the faithfulness of God. The expositors observe the nor to repent: his purpose unchanging. The expositors observe the hath he said: what he speaks he surely does. The expositors observe the the believer's rock: impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2). The believer is taught to mark the not a man to lie, the nor to repent, the hath he said, the the believer's rock, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that God is not a man, that he should lie or repent — the rock under all his promises, the believer's consolation, for it is impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2).
20:
"Behold, I have received commandment to bless: and he hath blessed; and I cannot reverse it." The witnesses note Balaam's confession — he has received commandment to bless, and cannot reverse what God has blessed. The witnesses note that Gill notes he had received commandment to bless the people of Israel, to declare them happy. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Balaam meets Balak's expectation that he would take back the blessing with the plain declaration, "I cannot reverse it." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the commandment to bless — balaam bound to pronounce blessing; the he hath blessed — god himself having blessed Israel; the I cannot reverse it — the curse made impossible; the no power can reverse it — what God blesses is blessed indeed (Rom. 11:29).

The witnesses agree the diviner owns his utter powerlessness against God's blessing: commanded to bless, and seeing that God has blessed, he confesses he cannot turn it back — the curse for hire is impossible; the words are the surrender of every enemy before the irrevocable favour of God toward his own, and the comfort of the church, that the blessing God has pronounced no power in earth or hell can reverse, for what God blesses is blessed indeed (cf. Rom. 11:29).

So the witnesses make the verse the irreversible blessing: The witnesses note the commandment to bless: Balaam bound to pronounce blessing. The witnesses note the he hath blessed: God himself having blessed Israel. The witnesses note the I cannot reverse it: the curse made impossible. The witnesses note the no power can reverse it: what God blesses is blessed indeed (Rom. 11:29). The expositors observe the commandment to bless: Balaam bound to pronounce blessing. The expositors observe the he hath blessed: God himself having blessed Israel. The expositors observe the I cannot reverse it: the curse made impossible. The expositors observe the no power can reverse it: what God blesses is blessed indeed (Rom. 11:29). The believer is taught to mark the commandment to bless, the he hath blessed, the I cannot reverse it, the no power can reverse it, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balaam confessed he could not reverse God's blessing — the surrender of every enemy before the irrevocable favour of God, what he blesses blessed indeed (Rom. 11:29).
21:
"He hath not beheld iniquity in Jacob, neither hath he seen perverseness in Israel: the LORD his God is with him, and the shout of a king is among them." The witnesses note God not beholding iniquity in Jacob — and the LORD his God with him, the shout of a king among them. The witnesses note that Gill notes this means not that there was no sin in them, but that God did not mark it in strict justice to punish, but forgave and did not impute it; the Targums restrain it to idolatry, none worshipping idols. The witnesses note that the LORD their God was with them, and the joyful shout of a king among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not beheld iniquity — sin not imputed in strict justice; the not seen perverseness — covered and forgiven, the face turned away; the the LORD with him — god's presence their defence; the justification — blessed is he to whom sin is not imputed (Rom. 4:8).

The witnesses agree the words breathe the gospel of justification: God "beheld not iniquity in Jacob" not because they had none, but because he did not impute it in strict justice — covered and forgiven, his face turned from it, so that no charge could be laid; it is the very doctrine the apostle preaches, "blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin" (Rom. 4:8), and the ground of their safety is added — the LORD their God is with them, his presence their defence and their king.

So the witnesses make the verse no iniquity beheld: The witnesses note the not beheld iniquity: sin not imputed in strict justice. The witnesses note the not seen perverseness: covered and forgiven, the face turned away. The witnesses note the the LORD with him: God's presence their defence. The witnesses note the justification: blessed is he to whom sin is not imputed (Rom. 4:8). The expositors observe the not beheld iniquity: sin not imputed in strict justice. The expositors observe the not seen perverseness: covered and forgiven, the face turned away. The expositors observe the the LORD with him: God's presence their defence. The expositors observe the justification: blessed is he to whom sin is not imputed (Rom. 4:8). The believer is taught to mark the not beheld iniquity, the not seen perverseness, the the LORD with him, the justification, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that God beheld no iniquity in Jacob — sin not imputed but covered, the gospel of justification (Rom. 4:8), the LORD their God with them their defence and king.
22:
"God brought them out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn." The witnesses note God who brought Israel out of Egypt, their strength like that of the wild ox. The witnesses note that Gill notes God who brought them out of Egypt with a mighty hand will conduct them through the wilderness and bring them safe to Canaan, and will not suffer them to perish; it is vain to curse a people in such hands. The witnesses note that their strength is figured by the unicorn, the strong wild ox. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the God brought them out — the Exodus the pledge of their safety; the the strength of an unicorn — the wild ox, irresistible power; the will bring them in — he who delivered will surely keep; the against omnipotence — vain to curse a people in such hands.

The witnesses agree the appeal to the Exodus seals their security: the God who redeemed Israel from Egypt with great power will not now let them be cursed or destroyed, but carries them on with the strength of the wild ox — irresistible; the argument is the believer's own, that he who began the good work will finish it, the God who delivered will surely keep, so that to curse a people God has brought out and goes before is to fight against omnipotence.

So the witnesses make the verse brought out of Egypt: The witnesses note the God brought them out: the Exodus the pledge of their safety. The witnesses note the the strength of an unicorn: the wild ox, irresistible power. The witnesses note the will bring them in: he who delivered will surely keep. The witnesses note the against omnipotence: vain to curse a people in such hands. The expositors observe the God brought them out: the Exodus the pledge of their safety. The expositors observe the the strength of an unicorn: the wild ox, irresistible power. The expositors observe the will bring them in: he who delivered will surely keep. The expositors observe the against omnipotence: vain to curse a people in such hands. The believer is taught to mark the God brought them out, the the strength of an unicorn, the will bring them in, the against omnipotence, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that God brought Israel out of Egypt with the strength of the wild ox — the Exodus the pledge of their keeping, vain to curse a people God delivered and goes before.
23:
"Surely there is no enchantment against Jacob, neither is any divination against Israel: according to this time it shall be said of Jacob and of Israel, What hath God wrought!" The witnesses note Balaam owning that no enchantment or divination can prevail against Israel — what hath God wrought! The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam here confesses all his enchantments and divinations signified nothing and could never bring a curse on Israel; it was vain for him to use them, and vain for Balak to expect anything. The witnesses note that the wonder of God's work would be told — "What hath God wrought!" The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no enchantment — the diviner's arts confessed vain; the no divination against Israel — no spell touching God's people; the what hath God wrought — the nations to marvel at his work; the no weapon shall prosper — god's triumph over the powers of darkness (Isa. 54:17).

The witnesses agree the master enchanter confessed the bankruptcy of his arts before God's people: no spell, no divination, could touch a people the LORD had blessed, and instead of a curse the nations would marvel at what God had done for Israel; the verse is the triumph of God over all the powers of darkness arrayed against his own, and a standing assurance that no weapon or enchantment formed against them shall prosper (cf. Isa. 54:17).

So the witnesses make the verse no enchantment prevails: The witnesses note the no enchantment: the diviner's arts confessed vain. The witnesses note the no divination against Israel: no spell touching God's people. The witnesses note the what hath God wrought: the nations to marvel at his work. The witnesses note the no weapon shall prosper: God's triumph over the powers of darkness (Isa. 54:17). The expositors observe the no enchantment: the diviner's arts confessed vain. The expositors observe the no divination against Israel: no spell touching God's people. The expositors observe the what hath God wrought: the nations to marvel at his work. The expositors observe the no weapon shall prosper: God's triumph over the powers of darkness (Isa. 54:17). The believer is taught to mark the no enchantment, the no divination against Israel, the what hath God wrought, the no weapon shall prosper, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that no enchantment can prevail against Israel — the master diviner confessing his arts bankrupt, the triumph of God over all the powers arrayed against his own (Isa. 54:17).
24:
"Behold, the people shall rise up as a great lion, and lift up himself as a young lion: he shall not lie down until he eat of the prey..." The witnesses note Israel figured as a lion, rising up strong and victorious over its enemies. The witnesses note that Gill reads it of the lioness, the strongest and most warlike beast, fierce and furious. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the people rising up as a great lion to its prey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as a great lion — the strongest and most warlike beast; the lift up himself — rising strong and victorious; the eat of the prey — prevailing over its enemies; the the Lion of Judah — the figure fulfilled in Christ (Rev. 5:5).

The witnesses agree the oracle ends the second parable on a note of conquest: the people Balak hoped to curse are likened to a lion that will not rest until it has overcome its prey — a figure of Israel's coming victories over the nations; the image of the lion, taken up again of Judah and at last of Christ, "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" (Rev. 5:5), declares that God's people, far from being cursed, will prevail, the would-be curser foretelling their triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse the lion of Israel: The witnesses note the as a great lion: the strongest and most warlike beast. The witnesses note the lift up himself: rising strong and victorious. The witnesses note the eat of the prey: prevailing over its enemies. The witnesses note the the Lion of Judah: the figure fulfilled in Christ (Rev. 5:5). The expositors observe the as a great lion: the strongest and most warlike beast. The expositors observe the lift up himself: rising strong and victorious. The expositors observe the eat of the prey: prevailing over its enemies. The expositors observe the the Lion of Judah: the figure fulfilled in Christ (Rev. 5:5). The believer is taught to mark the as a great lion, the lift up himself, the eat of the prey, the the Lion of Judah, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Israel is figured as a lion that conquers its prey — the people Balak would curse foretold to prevail, the figure of the Lion of Judah fulfilled in Christ (Rev. 5:5).
25:
"And Balak said unto Balaam, Neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all." The witnesses note Balak's exasperation — if Balaam will not curse, let him at least not bless. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balak meant it would be as well to do nothing as to do what he did; some render it, "if thou wilt not curse, at least do not bless." The witnesses note that the king was galled by the repeated blessings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the neither curse — unable to get the curse he sought; the nor bless at all — galled by the repeated blessings; the rather silence — begging Balaam to hold his peace; the frustrated malice — the enemy reduced to a plea for silence.

The witnesses agree the king's cry is the petulance of frustrated malice: unable to get a curse, he would rather have silence than blessing, begging Balaam at least to hold his peace; the words betray how much the repeated blessings stung, and how helpless Balak was — reduced to asking the prophet merely to stop, since he could not make him curse, the enemy beaten down to a plea for silence.

So the witnesses make the verse neither curse nor bless: The witnesses note the neither curse: unable to get the curse he sought. The witnesses note the nor bless at all: galled by the repeated blessings. The witnesses note the rather silence: begging Balaam to hold his peace. The witnesses note the frustrated malice: the enemy reduced to a plea for silence. The expositors observe the neither curse: unable to get the curse he sought. The expositors observe the nor bless at all: galled by the repeated blessings. The expositors observe the rather silence: begging Balaam to hold his peace. The expositors observe the frustrated malice: the enemy reduced to a plea for silence. The believer is taught to mark the neither curse, the nor bless at all, the rather silence, the frustrated malice, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balak begged Balaam neither to curse nor bless — the petulance of frustrated malice, the enemy beaten down to a plea for mere silence.
26:
"But Balaam answered and said unto Balak, Told not I thee, saying, All that the LORD speaketh, that I must do?" The witnesses note Balaam pleading his consistency — he had warned from the first that he must speak only what the LORD said. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam appeals for his honesty and faithfulness, that when they first met he plainly told Balak what to expect. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read his answer — told he not him that all the LORD speaks, that he must do? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the told not I thee — appealing to his consistency; the all that the LORD speaketh — only God's word to be spoken; the that I must do — bound by a power he could not break; the honest against his will — god holding a false heart to the truth.

The witnesses agree the prophet shelters behind a true principle he never truly loved: he had indeed warned Balak that he could speak only God's word, and now pleads it to excuse himself; yet his very faithfulness in this was forced, not chosen, the covetous man bound by a power he could not break, made honest against his will — a reminder that God can hold even a false heart to the truth, compelling the unwilling to confess and obey his word.

So the witnesses make the verse Balaam's plea: The witnesses note the told not I thee: appealing to his consistency. The witnesses note the all that the LORD speaketh: only God's word to be spoken. The witnesses note the that I must do: bound by a power he could not break. The witnesses note the honest against his will: God holding a false heart to the truth. The expositors observe the told not I thee: appealing to his consistency. The expositors observe the all that the LORD speaketh: only God's word to be spoken. The expositors observe the that I must do: bound by a power he could not break. The expositors observe the honest against his will: God holding a false heart to the truth. The believer is taught to mark the told not I thee, the all that the LORD speaketh, the that I must do, the honest against his will, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balaam pleaded he had warned he must speak only God's word — a true principle forced on a covetous heart, God holding the unwilling to confess and obey his word.
27:
"And Balak said unto Balaam, Come, I pray thee, I will bring thee unto another place; peradventure it will please God that thou mayest curse me them from thence." The witnesses note Balak's third attempt — to a more religious place, hoping God might yet permit the curse. The witnesses note that Gill notes he would bring him to another place, if not better for the view, yet more religious, on which account he hoped for success. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Balak now names God as Elohim, seeming satisfied that Balaam was hindered by God from cursing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unto another place — a more religious spot chosen; the peradventure it will please God — hoping God might yet permit it; the owning God hinders — balak naming Elohim as the obstacle; the clutching at superstition — as if the unchangeable could be coaxed.

The witnesses agree the king's obstinacy clutches at superstition to the last: now owning that it was God who hindered the curse, Balak yet imagines that a holier spot might "please God" to change his mind — as if the unchangeable could be coaxed by a change of place; the third attempt, like the others, rests on the folly that the divine will toward Israel might somehow be reversed, the enemy unable to learn that what God has blessed he will not curse.

So the witnesses make the verse the third attempt: The witnesses note the unto another place: a more religious spot chosen. The witnesses note the peradventure it will please God: hoping God might yet permit it. The witnesses note the owning God hinders: Balak naming Elohim as the obstacle. The witnesses note the clutching at superstition: as if the unchangeable could be coaxed. The expositors observe the unto another place: a more religious spot chosen. The expositors observe the peradventure it will please God: hoping God might yet permit it. The expositors observe the owning God hinders: Balak naming Elohim as the obstacle. The expositors observe the clutching at superstition: as if the unchangeable could be coaxed. The believer is taught to mark the unto another place, the peradventure it will please God, the owning God hinders, the clutching at superstition, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balak tried a third place, hoping God might be pleased to permit the curse — clutching at superstition, unable to learn that what God has blessed he will not curse.
28:
"And Balak brought Balaam unto the top of Peor, that looketh toward Jeshimon." The witnesses note the move to the top of Peor, a height of Baal-worship, looking toward the desert. The witnesses note that Gill notes Peor was a high mountain in Moab where the idol Baal was worshipped, whence Baal-peor had his name, and probably a temple of Bethpeor stood. The witnesses note that it looked toward Jeshimon, the wilderness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the top of Peor — the high place of Baal-worship; the toward Jeshimon — looking out over the wilderness; the the seat of Baal — as if the idol's nearness might help; the a dark irony — the place where Israel would later be ensnared (Num. 25:3).

The witnesses agree the choice of Peor, the seat of Baal-worship, marks the depth of Balak's superstition: he brought Balaam to the very high place of his god, as if the nearness of the idol might win what two attempts had failed to gain; the irony is dark, for it was at this same Baal-peor that Israel would soon be ensnared by the counsel Balaam left behind — the place of the third failed curse becoming the place of a subtler temptation (cf. Num. 25:3).

So the witnesses make the verse the top of Peor: The witnesses note the the top of Peor: the high place of Baal-worship. The witnesses note the toward Jeshimon: looking out over the wilderness. The witnesses note the the seat of Baal: as if the idol's nearness might help. The witnesses note the a dark irony: the place where Israel would later be ensnared (Num. 25:3). The expositors observe the the top of Peor: the high place of Baal-worship. The expositors observe the toward Jeshimon: looking out over the wilderness. The expositors observe the the seat of Baal: as if the idol's nearness might help. The expositors observe the a dark irony: the place where Israel would later be ensnared (Num. 25:3). The believer is taught to mark the the top of Peor, the toward Jeshimon, the the seat of Baal, the a dark irony, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balak brought Balaam to the top of Peor, the seat of Baal-worship — the depth of his superstition, and the dark place where Israel would soon be ensnared (Num. 25:3).
29:
"And Balaam said unto Balak, Build me here seven altars, and prepare me here seven bullocks and seven rams." The witnesses note Balaam ordering, a third time, seven altars and the same sacrifices. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam was willing to try again, to gratify the king, and especially to get the wages of unrighteousness, which he dearly loved (2 Pet. 2:15). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the sacrifices were the same as in the former cases. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven altars again — the same ritual a third time; the seven bullocks and rams — the costly sacrifices renewed; the the wages of unrighteousness — which Balaam dearly loved (2 Pet. 2:15); the persisting for gain — held to a hopeless errand by the love of money.

The witnesses agree the third repetition lays bare Balaam's heart: he knew by now he could not curse, yet went through the whole costly ritual again, "to get the wages of unrighteousness," which, the apostle says, he loved (2 Pet. 2:15); the picture is of a man who against all light persists in the way of gain, hoping somehow to wring a profit from sin — the love of money holding him to an errand he knew was hopeless and hateful to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the third altars: The witnesses note the seven altars again: the same ritual a third time. The witnesses note the seven bullocks and rams: the costly sacrifices renewed. The witnesses note the the wages of unrighteousness: which Balaam dearly loved (2 Pet. 2:15). The witnesses note the persisting for gain: held to a hopeless errand by the love of money. The expositors observe the seven altars again: the same ritual a third time. The expositors observe the seven bullocks and rams: the costly sacrifices renewed. The expositors observe the the wages of unrighteousness: which Balaam dearly loved (2 Pet. 2:15). The expositors observe the persisting for gain: held to a hopeless errand by the love of money. The believer is taught to mark the seven altars again, the seven bullocks and rams, the the wages of unrighteousness, the persisting for gain, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balaam ordered seven altars a third time — persisting against all light for the wages of unrighteousness he loved (2 Pet. 2:15), held to a hopeless errand by the love of money.
30:
"And Balak did as Balaam had said, and offered a bullock and a ram on every altar." The witnesses note Balak, sparing no cost, offering once more a bullock and a ram on every altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes that though the sacrifices were expensive, Balak did not grudge them, sparing no cost to gain his point, though he could now have little hope of it. The witnesses note that he offered, as before, on every altar. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Balak did as said — sparing no cost to the end; the a bullock and a ram — the offerings renewed once more; the little hope — yet unwilling to give up his malice; the vain to the last — the blessing not to be turned to a curse.

The witnesses agree the closing verse seals the futility of the whole enterprise: Balak, though by now almost hopeless, still poured out his costly sacrifices, unwilling to give up his malice while a chance remained; the lavish but vain offerings are the fitting end of the chapter, a monument to the obstinacy of those who set themselves against God's people — spending and laboring to no purpose, for the blessing of God could not be turned into a curse.

So the witnesses make the verse the last sacrifices: The witnesses note the Balak did as said: sparing no cost to the end. The witnesses note the a bullock and a ram: the offerings renewed once more. The witnesses note the little hope: yet unwilling to give up his malice. The witnesses note the vain to the last: the blessing not to be turned to a curse. The expositors observe the Balak did as said: sparing no cost to the end. The expositors observe the a bullock and a ram: the offerings renewed once more. The expositors observe the little hope: yet unwilling to give up his malice. The expositors observe the vain to the last: the blessing not to be turned to a curse. The believer is taught to mark the Balak did as said, the a bullock and a ram, the little hope, the vain to the last, and to rest in the faithful God who blesses. The verse thus records that Balak offered the costly sacrifices a third time, almost hopeless yet unwilling to yield — the vain end of the enterprise, the blessing of God not to be turned into a curse.
